case_name: Oxamyl removal from surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for oxamyl-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply under a minimal-chemical-use preference.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal utility draws raw water from a river-reservoir system affected by seasonal agricultural runoff. Monitoring has identified oxamyl as the clearly dominant organic contaminant of concern, while general surface-water quality remains manageable with standard clarification and filtration. The utility wants a proven treatment approach for drinking water that minimizes chemical addition and avoids unnecessarily complex operation.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable oxamyl reduction suitable for drinking water production while meeting normal finished-water quality expectations for surface-water supplies.
secondary_goals: use established and supportable municipal treatment technology
maintain moderate energy demand and straightforward operation
preserve robustness during seasonal runoff events

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove coarse debris and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.biofiltration_or_dual_media_filtration: reduce suspended solids and provide stable pretreatment for adsorptive polishing
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb oxamyl and other trace organics with minimal chemical addition
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbial protection for drinking water

applicability_rationale: oxamyl is a dissolved organic micropollutant for which activated carbon is a well-established control approach in drinking water treatment
the case explicitly favors minimal chemical use, which supports adsorption-based treatment over chemically intensive oxidation or coagulation-first strategies
surface water turbidity is moderate rather than extreme, so filtration followed by GAC is practical and proven
GAC can be integrated into municipal treatment with moderate operating complexity and good robustness
potential_risks: natural organic matter can compete for adsorption sites and shorten GAC bed life
breakthrough monitoring is needed to ensure oxamyl removal remains reliable
pretreatment must control solids to avoid premature headloss or carbon fouling

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove debris and stabilize raw water feed
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: adsorb dissolved oxamyl during contact time prior to separation
core_unit_functions.solid_liquid_separation_or_filtration: remove PAC particles and associated contaminants
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished water

applicability_rationale: PAC is a conventional treatment option for episodic pesticide contamination in surface waters
it can be particularly useful for seasonal runoff events where contaminant spikes occur
chemical use remains moderate compared with advanced oxidation because the main treatment reagent is adsorbent rather than oxidant
the process is familiar to many municipal plants and can be deployed flexibly
potential_risks: performance depends strongly on PAC dose, contact time, and natural organic matter competition
continuous solids separation is required after PAC addition
carbon handling and dust control add operational needs

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove large debris and protect downstream equipment
core_unit_functions.biological_filtration: remove oxidation byproducts and polish biodegradable organics

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation is technically capable of degrading many pesticide-type organic contaminants including carbamate compounds under suitable design conditions
biological filtration is a common downstream polishing step after oxidation in drinking water treatment
this route can be considered when direct adsorption alone is insufficient or when broader organic control is also desired
potential_risks: the process conflicts somewhat with the minimal-chemical-use preference because oxidant and potentially peroxide addition are required
oxidation efficiency depends on water matrix effects and reactor control
byproduct formation and downstream biodegradation control must be managed

core_unit_functions.coagulation: destabilize colloids and reduce particulate and natural organic matter load
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove formed flocs prior to filtration

applicability_rationale: this is a robust conventional-plus-GAC train for surface water when turbidity variability is a concern
coagulation can help reduce natural organic matter that would otherwise compete with oxamyl for GAC sites
the overall train is highly proven in municipal practice
potential_risks: this option uses more chemicals than preferred because it relies on coagulant addition
sludge production and handling burden are higher than for simpler adsorption-led trains
added conventional units may be unnecessary if raw water solids remain only moderate

core_unit_functions.low_pressure_membrane_or_reverse_osmosis: provide physical separation, with reverse osmosis offering stronger dissolved contaminant rejection
core_unit_functions.stabilization_if_needed: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment when required

applicability_rationale: high-pressure membrane treatment can reduce a broad range of dissolved contaminants including pesticides
it may be selected where multiple difficult contaminants are present or very high removal margins are required
potential_risks: this is unnecessarily energy-intensive for a single oxamyl-focused case with a minimal-chemical-use preference
concentrate management is a significant drawback
membrane fouling control and post-treatment needs increase operational burden
